headline: Nothing's fully original — remix your lineage honest
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon said it best—
Nothing's fully original, we just remix the lineage, honest.
Steal like an artist... let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Nothing's fully original, that's the truth we inherit,
Remix your lineage, make it honest, no counterfeit.
From the ancestors' bars to the pixels on my screen,
Every idea's a seed—water it, let it breathe.
Steal like an artist, transform what you admire,
Mash it with your fire, take it higher, never tire.
In the age of AI and infinite scrolls,
Remix your bloodline, claim your soul!
[Verse 1]
Back in the day, Picasso peeped the Africans, flipped the forms,
Shakespeare borrowed plots, made 'em his own norms.
T.S. Eliot said immature poets imitate,
Mature ones steal—then elevate the state.
Kleon dropped the book: "Nothing comes from nowhere,"
Every track, every app, every fresh idea's a remix affair.
You a product of the books you read, the beats you bang,
The family stories, the late-night YouTube gang.
My Vietnamese roots in the phở broth wisdom,
Mixed with Bay Area hustle—Silicon vision.
Track timing apps from old stopwatches and Supabase dreams,
PE lessons turned code—remixing the team.
Don't front like you invented the wheel,
Honor the lineage, that's the real deal.
Copy your heroes till you find your twist,
Then break the mold— that's how legends persist.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Scroll the feed, but curate the feast,
AI spits drafts? Good—now remix 'em with your beast.
Kid drawing prompts from old masters + Pokémon flair,
Family tree app with Harada grids in the layers.
Music industry? Samples been the game since '88,
Kanye flipped soul, now Grok helps generate.
TikTok dances? Straight lineage from breakbeats and vogue,
Memes evolve daily—remix or get rogue.
Day trading charts? Patterns from generations past,
But your edge is the psychology, emotions you mash.
Don't plagiarize—transform, credit the source,
Build in public, show the lineage, stay on course.
In schools, apps, and startups, same rule applies:
Take the best of what's been, synthesize.
No more paralysis from "gotta be 100% new,"
Embrace the mashup—watch your vision breakthrough.
[Bridge] (Slower, reflective—maybe with vocal chops)
Honest thieves don't hide the scars...
We wear 'em proud, connect the stars...
From grandma's tales to coder bars...
Remix your lineage, that's who we are.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So to the creators in Milpitas grinding late,
Teachers building tools, parents flipping fate.
Son's vocal tic? Turn pain into rhythm and grace,
Wife's support in the background—lineage embrace.
Steal from Kleon, from Ohtani's grind, from every app that came before,
Then drop something fresh that opens new doors.
Nothing's fully original—own that fact,
Remix honest, stay intact.
Lineage loud, future stacked...
Yeah, steal like an artist—now go make it crack.

headline: Steal structures and thinking, not just surface style
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon in Steal Like an Artist—
Don't just copy the paint; steal the thinking, the structure, the artist.
Surface style is cheap—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Steal the structure, not the surface flash,
Copy how they think, not the Instagram stash.
Bones before skin, process before pose,
That's how your own voice quietly grows.
Don't cosplay the outfit, study the map,
Steal the architecture—then add your slap.
Style fades fast; systems compound,
Steal like a thinker—build on solid ground.
[Verse 1]
Kleon says: copy the heroes, but dig past the look,
How they arrange time, how they keep a notebook.
Not the font they use—how they choose what to cut,
Not the logo glow—how they decide what is enough.
Picasso's geometry wasn't a filter on a phone,
It was a way of seeing form, stripping it to bone.
Basquiat's crowns weren't stickers for your brand deck—
They were code for power, royalty under stress and sweat.
When you only steal surface, you stay a cover band,
Playing someone else's set with a weaker hand.
Steal their questions, their constraints, their daily drill,
How they fail, how they revise, how they sit still.
Genealogy of taste: who taught the one you love?
Follow that tree up—that's the real push and shove.
Structures travel; costumes don't survive the wash,
Steal the skeleton—then hang your own flesh on it, boss.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: ChatGPT can mimic a voice in a click,
But if you only paste the tone, you never learn the trick.
Steal the prompt structure—how they frame the problem tight—
Not the pretty output that fools the team tonight.
Designer on Figma? Don't clone the purple gradient kit,
Steal how they grid, how they cut, how they quit.
Creator on TikTok? Not the dance, the hook architecture—
Three-second open, pay-off, loop—that's the real lecture.
Parent at the table? Don't copy Pinterest lunch art,
Steal the routine: phones down, one question from the heart.
Engineer shipping? Not their tech-bro hoodie drip—
Steal their PR habit: small diffs, clear commit, long trip.
Job interview? Don't parrot buzzwords from a thread,
Steal how good candidates structure stories instead.
Tomorrow morning: reverse-engineer one thing you love—
Write three rules they use. Run one. That's enough.
[Bridge]
Surface is rented...
Structure is owned...
When the trend dies...
Your bones still hold.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So stop screenshotting aesthetics like that's the craft,
Start mapping how they think—front, middle, and aft.
One notebook page: structure I stole, twist I own,
Do that for a week—watch your work come home.
Steal structures and thinking, not just surface style—
That's the grown-up version of the Kleon file.
Copy the map, walk your road, leave a trail they can feel...
Steal the bones—then make the body real.

headline: Build the thing you wish already existed
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon said it plain in Steal Like an Artist—
Build the thing you wish already existed. Start it.
Stop waiting for permission—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Build the thing you wish already lived,
Don't wait for the market to hand you a gift.
If it ain't on the shelf, that's your invitation,
Scratch your own itch—that's the purest motivation.
Make the tool, the book, the class, the room,
The app that saves you from the same old doom.
Wish is a seed; build is the root—
Stop browsing forever. Ship the proof.
[Verse 1]
Kleon's line cuts through the "someday" fog:
The best work solves a problem that haunted the dog—
You, the first user, the one who stayed up late
Looking for a product that never hit the gate.
He learned by making the art he wanted to see,
Not by chasing a niche from a growth-hack degree.
DIY is not a phase for broke kids only—
It's how culture gets made when the catalog's lonely.
Zines, blogs, home studios, basement code,
Whole scenes started 'cause nobody else showed.
You don't need a focus group to bless the spark,
You need the courage to build in the dark.
Wish without build is just elegant complaint,
Build without wish is busywork and paint.
Marry the ache to the action—that's the craft—
Make what you needed when nobody had your back.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: You hate your team's status chaos—
Build a one-page ritual, not another Slack embargos.
Kid can't find calm after school? Design a ten-minute kit—
Snack, stretch, one song, then homework—simple, lit.
AI tools flood the feed; none fit your PE class timing—
Hack a spreadsheet, then a tiny app—stop pining.
Newsletter you keep searching for? Write three issues yourself,
Don't wait for Substack royalty to clear the shelf.
Freelance rates feel broken? Publish your own rate card truth,
Templates that treat craft like more than youth.
Dating apps empty? Host a monthly dinner with friends who make—
Build community, not another swipe-and-wait.
That Notion setup you bookmark daily? Clone, then cut it down
To the five blocks you actually use in this town.
Tomorrow: list three things you still search for and never find—
Pick one. Build a ugly v1. Leave polish behind.
[Bridge]
The wishlist is a mirror...
Not a shopping cart...
What you keep missing...
Is where you start.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
Creators, teachers, parents, founders on the grind—
Stop waiting for someone else's product to mind.
Your frustration is a brief; treat it like a brief,
Ship something small that brings you quiet relief.
Build the thing you wish already existed—own that line,
Then improve it in public, one honest time.
Wish less. Build more. Use it yourself first...
If it saves you, it might save the next person worse.

headline: Use your hands before you live on screens
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon in Steal Like an Artist—
Use your hands before you live on screens. Feel it.
Analog first, then digital—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Hands before screens, paper before glass,
Sketch the thought before the app makes it fast.
Fingers in the real world learn what pixels hide,
Touch is a teacher the feed never provides.
Unplug the input, plug in the craft,
Body knows patterns the cursor can't draft.
Hands before screens—that's how ideas root—
Then bring 'em online sharp, not mute.
[Verse 1]
Kleon keeps a notebook, scissors, glue, a desk that stains,
Not because he's cute—because the hand rewires the brain.
He cuts newspapers, rearranges the day,
Collage as thinking, not as Pinterest play.
When everything starts in software, options multiply,
Paralysis dressed as "infinite tools" you try.
Paper has friction—and friction is a gift,
Limits force choices; choices make the work lift.
Writers longhand first, painters on scrap wood,
Chefs taste with hands, builders trust grain and good.
The screen is a publisher, not always a birthplace—
Birth needs mess: ink, dust, a physical space.
Steal Like an Artist ain't anti-tech—it's order:
Touch the idea, then tool it without disorder.
Your hands remember what your tabs forget—
Start there, then let the pixels finish the set.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Before you open Figma, draw boxes on paper,
Before the Notion board, sticky notes on the table later.
AI draft? Fine—but outline in a notebook first,
Handwrite three ugly sentences—break the thirst.
Kid stuck on homework in a Chrome tab maze—
Print the problem, solve with pencil, reclaim the days.
Meeting about to explode into ten docs and a thread?
Whiteboard five minutes, then type what was said.
Gym PR? Log it in a pocket book before the app streak,
Feel the lift; don't outsource the peak.
Side project? Wireframe on napkins, not twenty components—
Ship a cardboard prototype that actually comments.
Phone-free hour: scissors, paper, one idea to cut and paste—
You'll invent more than another doomscroll waste.
Tomorrow morning: ten minutes analog only—no glass—
Then open the laptop. Watch the quality pass.
[Bridge]
The hand is slow on purpose...
Slow is how you see...
Screens can amplify...
What the body set free.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So put the glass down before the thought is born,
Let your fingers argue with the page till form is worn.
Then digitize with intention, not as default mode—
Hands first, screens second—that's the Kleon code.
Use your hands before you live on screens—live it raw,
Collage, sketch, scribble, build what you saw.
Ink on the desk is proof you showed up today...
Touch the work—then let the pixels play.

headline: Side projects feed the main work
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon said it in Steal Like an Artist clear—
Side projects feed the main work. Keep 'em near.
Not a distraction—fuel. Let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Side projects feed the main, don't starve the side,
That's where the weird ideas go to survive.
Play is research dressed in after-hours clothes,
Hobby energy pays the day job what it owes.
Don't kill the side hustle of the soul for "focus,"
Cross-pollinate—watch the main work notice.
Side projects feed the main—keep the garden wild—
That's how the day job gets recompiled.
[Verse 1]
Kleon treats side projects like a second bloodstream,
Not guilt, not "wasted nights"—a legitimate scheme.
When the main gig locks you in a narrow lane,
The side work lets your taste run off the train.
Boredom at work is a signal, not a sin—
Go make something useless till the juice comes in.
History's full of day jobs funding night experiments:
Kafka at insurance, Einstein at the patent desk, no monuments
Built only in official hours. The side is where risk lives
Without the boardroom veto on what curiosity gives.
He says: don't quit your day job too early to "be free"—
Use stability to fund the art you need to see.
The side teaches courage, new tools, new friends,
Then smuggles those gifts back when the main work bends.
Feeding is the metaphor—starving the side starves both—
Protect the playground. That's the creative oath.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Your job is Salesforce and ticket queues—
Nights you build a tiny PE timer app—skills reuse.
Corporate decks feel dead? Weekend zine on local food,
Suddenly your slides have voice, humor, human mood.
Parenting is the main event? Side sketchbook after kids sleep
Keeps you a person, not a logistics heap.
AI day job writing emails? Side project: short films with your phone—
Story sense migrates; prompts get better alone.
Teacher by day, pottery on Sundays—hands learn patience
That classroom chaos can't fake with statements.
Don't post every side project like it's a brand pivot—
Some sides are just batteries. Guard that limit.
Calendar trick: one protected block labeled "useless make,"
No KPI, no LinkedIn—just for the sake.
This week: name one side thing that quietly feeds your craft—
Schedule it like a meeting. Don't let it get halfed.
[Bridge]
The main work pays the rent...
The side work pays the soul...
Starve either one...
And both feel cold.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So stop apologizing for the project that "doesn't scale,"
It's compost for the garden where your real work will sail.
Side projects feed the main—Kleon already wrote the bill—
Honor the side without abandoning the skill.
Protect one evening. Make something nobody asked for.
Bring the leftovers to work. Watch the door open more.
Feed the side, feed the main, keep both alive...
Play is not the enemy of work—it's the drive.

headline: Share for the right people, not for everyone
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon in Steal Like an Artist—
Share for the right people, not for everyone. Smartest.
Audience is a choice—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Not for everyone—for the right ones in the room,
I'd rather ten true fans than a viral boom that zooms
Past people who don't care and never will.
Share like a signal, not a spam bill.
Right people, right message, right pace—
Quiet loyalty beats loud empty space.
Share for the ones who get it—that's the aim—
Not the whole internet saying your name.
[Verse 1]
Kleon pushes against the myth of mass applause:
You don't need the planet; you need a cause
And a circle that actually uses what you make.
"Everyone" is a crowd that never stays awake.
He talks about finding your scene, your weird tribe—
People who steal from you back, who vibe
With the same questions, the same half-finished art.
That's not niche as failure—that's niche as start.
When you write for everyone, you sand off the edges,
Become wallpaper, polite, no ledges.
When you write for someone specific, the work gets teeth—
A real reader, a real problem underneath.
Ignore metrics that count strangers as success;
Count the replies that change how you obsess.
Share as hospitality for your people, not a billboard scream—
Invite the right guests. Leave the rest to their feed dream.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Stop optimizing every post for the algorithm gods—
Post for the five teachers who actually use your mods.
Newsletter? Write like you're emailing one sharp friend,
Not "valued subscribers" with a corporate bend.
Product launch? Talk to the users who filed the bugs you fixed,
Not a cold blast to a list that never clicked.
Kid's art? Share in the family chat and the school wall—
Not a public feed that invites cruel scroll.
Job hunt? Send the portfolio to three humans who know your craft,
Not spray applications till your soul goes soft.
TikTok can wait; Discord with your real peers might be the room—
Right people first, scale later if it blooms.
Mute the vanity metrics for one month. Track instead:
Who replied with work of their own? Who read and then led?
Tomorrow: pick one audience of ten. Make something only they need—
Send it direct. Watch the "everyone" greed recede.
[Bridge]
Everyone is nobody...
When the message is thin...
Someone is everything...
When the work lets them in.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So shrink the room on purpose, raise the standard inside,
Share for the right people—let the wrong ones slide.
Kleon's gift is permission to stop performing for the void—
Build for the few who make the silence less noise.
Name your ten. Serve them hard. Ignore the rest of the map...
Share for the right people—that's how scenes attach.
Not viral. Vital. Not famous. Found...
Right people only. Stand your ground.

headline: Build a scene where you are
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon said in Steal Like an Artist—
Build a scene where you are. Don't wait for the artist
City or the perfect network—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Build a scene where you are, don't wait for the coast,
Plant culture in your zip code—that's the boast.
Not Brooklyn or wherever the thread says is hot—
Your block, your school, your group chat, your lot.
Scene is made, not found on a plane ticket receipt—
Invite, host, share, repeat.
Build a scene where you are—start tonight—
Local magic is still magic when you get it right.
[Verse 1]
Kleon lived in a place that wasn't "the center,"
Still made work, still found people who'd enter
The conversation if you open the door first.
Waiting for New York is how decades get cursed.
A scene is shared attention plus shared making—
Not a skyline. Not a famous street for faking.
Coffee shops, libraries, basements, Discord rooms,
School parking lots after practice—those are looms.
He says hang around people more talented than you,
But "around" can mean your town if you do the work too:
Start the reading group, the critique night, the tiny show,
The newsletter that only locals know.
Geography matters less than generosity and grit—
Be the node. Be the host. Refuse to sit
And complain that "nothing happens here."
Something happens wherever somebody steers.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: You're in a suburb, not a cultural capital—
Host a monthly make-night: laptops, kids, snacks, capital
Of attention for two hours. That's a scene seed.
Teachers: lunch-table show-and-tell of what you read.
Remote workers: one café co-work block with rules—
No meetings, just presence—borrow each other's fuels.
Parents: swap skill nights—coding for sourdough, PE for art—
Build exchange, not isolation in the minivan cart.
Online counts if it's a real room with names and norms—
Not drive-by likes. Weekly voice. Shared forms.
Stop refreshing cities you don't live in for permission—
Post a flyer: "Bring a work-in-progress." That's the mission.
Map three people within thirty minutes who make things—
Text them this week. Propose a tiny gathering.
Scene doesn't need funding; it needs a first yes—
You can be that yes in a living room, no less.
[Bridge]
The center is a myth...
That keeps the edges quiet...
Start where your feet are...
Make the silence riot.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So cancel the fantasy that art only lives elsewhere,
Build a scene where you are—chair to chair.
Kleon's line is a local manifesto in plain clothes:
Be the reason your town has somewhere culture goes.
Invite two people. Make something. Invite two more...
Scenes compound. That's the score.
Where you are is enough if you show up loud...
Build the scene—right here, right now.

headline: Keep life steady so the work can go wild
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon in Steal Like an Artist—
Keep life steady so the work can go wild. Smartest balance.
Boring calendar, wild pages—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Steady life, wild work—that's the trade that pays,
Bills on time so the mind can misbehave.
Chaos at home kills chaos on the page,
You need a calm runway for the wild stage.
Don't romanticize the mess as "authentic fuel"—
Stability is the studio. That's the rule.
Keep life steady so the work can go wild—
Boring mornings, brave files.
[Verse 1]
Kleon argues against the myth of the suffering genius:
Broke, dramatic, always almost famous.
He wants a day job, a routine, a life that doesn't implode
Every time you need courage to walk a new road.
Wild work needs surplus—time, sleep, a little money,
Not constant crisis cosplay that ain't funny.
When your personal life is a dumpster fire show,
Creativity spends all day just trying to cope.
Steady doesn't mean small; it means reliable ground—
Same wake time, clean desk, people who stick around.
Constraints of a normal life aren't the enemy of art—
They're the frame that lets risk play a smarter part.
Steal the structure of a calm day, then go feral in the draft,
Not feral in your finances, relationships, craft.
The paradox holds: the wilder the work you want,
The quieter the life that should fund that hunt.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Stop treating burnout as a badge of honor online—
Sleep, groceries, calendar—that's the real design.
Side project late nights? Fine—protect the morning routine hard,
Kids' drop-off, one walk, inbox after the yard.
Creator economy says "hustle till you break"—
Kleon says keep the day job till the art can take
The weight without wrecking your health and home.
Freelance? Automate invoices; don't live in loan.
AI tools won't save a life with no boundaries set—
Mute Slack after dinner; let the wild draft frets
Inside a container: ninety minutes, phone in another room,
Then return to steady—laundry, dinner, no doom.
Couples: agree on one chaos-free night a week—
That's when the weird project gets to speak.
This week: pick one life leak (sleep, money, schedule) and seal it—
Then spend the surplus on work that can feel it.
[Bridge]
Wild needs a shoreline...
Or it floods the house...
Steady is not selling out...
It's keeping the lights on for the mouse
That writes at midnight...
And still stands for the spouse.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So quit confusing self-destruction with depth of soul,
Keep life steady so the work can go wild—that's the goal.
Pay the bills, hug your people, protect the sleep,
Then on the page? Go deep, go strange, go steep.
Kleon gave permission to be a boring genius on purpose...
Steady life. Wild work. That's the service.
Tomorrow: one stabilizing move, one brave creative risk—
Do both. That's the mix.

headline: Creativity is subtraction as much as addition
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon in Steal Like an Artist—
Creativity is subtraction as much as addition. Cut with heart.
More isn't merrier—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Creativity is subtraction—cut to the bone,
What you remove can make the work alone
Stand clearer than a pile of "one more idea."
Less noise, more signal—that's the career.
Add with courage, subtract with taste,
Empty space is part of what you make.
Creativity is subtraction—own the delete—
The masterpiece might be what's missing from the sheet.
[Verse 1]
Kleon works with constraints: blackout poems, limited tools,
He blacks out newspapers—creativity by removal rules.
Not "what can I pile on?" but "what can I erase
Till only the charged words hold the space?"
Addition is easy in a culture of more—
More tabs, more features, more tracks on the floor.
Subtraction is the adult skill: kill your darlings, kill the clutter,
Kill the paragraph that only serves your ego's butter.
Editors know this; designers know white space;
Chefs know reduction—sauce that earned its place.
The book says your job is also what you refuse—
Influences you drop, projects you don't choose.
A full life of inputs without ruthless cut
Is a landfill of almosts, never a what.
Steal the cut: collage is as much discard as glue—
What falls away is how the true shows through.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Your slide deck has forty slides—cut to twelve,
Say one thing well; leave the rest on the shelf.
App roadmap bloated? Ship three features that hurt to choose—
Delete the nice-to-haves that users never use.
AI will generate infinite options if you let it—
Your job is the cut: pick, refine, forget it.
Closet, calendar, camera roll—same law applies:
Subtraction is creativity with adult eyes.
Writing a post? Draft long, publish short—
One sharp claim beats a thread that goes soft in court.
Kid's schedule packed with "enrichment"? Subtract one thing—
Boredom is a studio; space makes imagination swing.
Design system: fewer components, stronger rules—
Restraint is how brands stop looking like fools.
Tomorrow: open one project and remove twenty percent—
Words, features, commitments. Feel the ascent.
[Bridge]
The delete key is a brush...
Silence is a note...
What you leave out...
Is how the kept lines float.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So stop measuring creativity by how much you add,
Measure it by clarity after the bad
Gets cut. Kleon's blackout poems were a sermon in ink—
Creativity is subtraction—think, then shrink.
Add when you must, subtract when you can...
Empty is not empty when it serves the plan.
Tonight: one cut that scares you a little—make it...
What's left will be the work. Don't fake it.

headline: Leave breadcrumbs of how you work
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon in Steal Like an Artist—
Leave breadcrumbs of how you work. Share the process artist.
Not just the trophy—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Leave breadcrumbs of how you work, not just the win,
Show the path so others can walk in.
Sketches, notes, false starts, the ugly middle—
That's the map that makes the finished less a riddle.
Breadcrumbs, not billboards—small and true,
Process trails teach more than the final view.
Leave breadcrumbs of how you work—drop the trail—
Someone behind you needs that rail.
[Verse 1]
Kleon's whole vibe is generous theft with receipts:
If you learned in public scraps, leave those streets
Marked for the next person climbing the same hill.
Don't hoard the method like a secret skill
That dies with you. Document the way you steal,
The books you loved, the desks, the daily deal.
Breadcrumbs are humble: a photo of the draft,
A list of influences, a "here's what failed" graph.
Not a guru course—just honest crumbs on the forest floor
So the journey is legible, not a myth of "born for."
When masters hide the mess, juniors think they're broken—
Breadcrumbs say: mess is normal, keep going.
Steal Like an Artist is itself a breadcrumb book—
Simple drawings, plain talk, look how I look
At influence. Pay that forward in your own craft:
Leave marks. Leave methods. Leave the raft.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Shipping code? Write the README like a human,
Not only the API—why you chose this arrangement, the summation.
Designer? Post the three rejected frames with one sentence each—
Breadcrumbs beat a Dribbble hero shot that overreaches.
Teacher? Share the lesson that flopped and how you fixed the hook—
Students learn resilience when the process is a book.
Parent building a habit app? Log the week you almost quit—
That's the crumb that keeps another parent lit.
AI workflow? Don't just drop the final image flex—
Show the prompt chain, the rejects, the human checks.
LinkedIn loves polished wins; your blog can love the trail—
One weekly "how I worked" note is enough detail.
Git commits with real messages are breadcrumbs too—
"Tried X, failed, switched to Y" teaches the crew.
Tomorrow: publish one crumb—photo, paragraph, or short clip—
Of how you work, not only what you shipped.
[Bridge]
The finish line lies...
Without the middle miles...
Breadcrumbs tell the truth...
In ordinary styles.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So stop waiting for a masterpiece before you teach,
Leave breadcrumbs of how you work—within arm's reach.
Kleon's gift is process as public good, not ego play—
Drop the trail so the next thief can steal your way
And transform it further. That's the lineage stack...
Breadcrumbs today. Someone's map tomorrow. Pay it back.
One crumb before bed. Then another. Then a path...
Leave how you work—that's the aftermath that lasts.

headline: You need not be a genius to share process
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon in Show Your Work—
You need not be a genius to share process. That's the core.
Amateurs teach too—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
You don't need to be a genius to show the work,
Process from a learner still has worth.
Share the climb, not only the summit view—
Somebody two steps back needs you.
Not a guru, not a brand, just a human mid-make—
Generosity over genius—that's the stake.
You need not be a genius to share process—open the door—
The room gets smarter when we show more.
[Verse 1]
Show Your Work is Kleon's sequel energy: stop hiding,
You don't wait till mastery to start confiding
How you did the thing. "Amateur" means lover—
Love is enough credential to uncover
Your steps for others. Genius is a trap that freezes hands:
"I'm not ready" is how knowledge never lands
In the commons. He pushes you to document the middle,
The research, the mess—not a TED-talk riddle.
Think of open kitchens, lab notebooks, teachers who think aloud—
Process shared is how a craft becomes a crowd.
You are always someone's "further along,"
Even if you're still wrong half the day, still strong
Enough to say: here's what I tried, here's what failed,
Here's the link, here's the book that helped me sail.
That's not arrogance. That's citizenship in the scene—
Genius optional. Generosity on the routine.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: You're three months into learning Figma—
Post the file structure you finally understand, not a prima
Portfolio only. Junior dev? Write "how I debugged X"—
Seniors forget; beginners collect those texts.
Parent figuring sleep training? Share the log without the flex—
Another parent at 2 a.m. needs the next steps, not a perfect success.
AI user, not AI expert? Publish the prompt that finally worked
And the three that wasted an hour—truth over smirk.
Fitness? You're not a coach—still film the form check you needed—
Process crumbs from intermediates are undefeated.
Mute the voice that says "wait until you're impressive"—
Post one process note a week. Keep it progressive.
LinkedIn polish culture lies; real help is mid-journey clarity—
"Here's my checklist after I kept failing" is charity.
Tomorrow: teach one small thing you learned this month—no hype—
You need not be a genius. Just turn on the light.
[Bridge]
Genius is a story...
Process is a gift...
Share from where you stand...
Watch the whole room lift.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So drop the wait-for-expert costume at the door,
You need not be a genius to share process anymore.
Kleon wrote it so you'd stop hoarding half-learned gold—
Pass it on unfinished. Let the network hold
What you know so far. Update it later. Stay human mid-make...
Show your work before you're "great."
One process share this week. Name the tool, the fail, the fix...
Not genius. Generous. That's the mix.

headline: Share fragments and drafts, not only launches
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon in Show Your Work said—
Share fragments and drafts, not only launches. Spread
The middle, not just the ribbon cut—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Fragments and drafts, not only the launch day glow,
Show the half-built so the whole path can show.
Launches are rare; process is daily bread—
Share the sketch, the stub, the almost-said.
Don't wait for perfect packaging to speak—
A fragment can feed somebody this week.
Share fragments and drafts—open the WIP—
Finished is fine; unfinished is fellowship.
[Verse 1]
Kleon wants you to think of sharing as a habit, not an event,
Not one big drop while the rest of the year is spent
In secret. Fragments are shorthand for "work in progress honest"—
A sentence, a photo of the desk, a question you still contest.
Drafts invite collaboration; launches invite judgment only—
One is a conversation, the other can feel lonely
Even with applause. He learned audiences want the making,
The raw material, the almosts worth taking
Seriously. Think serial comics, open-source commits,
Writers posting chapters, chefs plating tests that don't hit—
Culture advances on drafts in public air,
Not only on polished products with press to spare.
If you only share launches, you train people to wait
For fireworks—and you train yourself to stagnate
Between them. Fragments keep the channel warm and true—
Here's where I am. Here's what I'm trying to do.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Building an app? Ship a Loom of the broken flow,
Not only the Product Hunt day with confetti in tow.
Writing a book? Share one messy page, not the preorder bomb—
Readers who care will help you stay calm and strong.
Course creators: post the outline before the funnels and ads—
Fragments filter for students who want the real labs.
Kid's science fair? Photograph the failed volcano first—
Drafts teach science better than the ribbon burst.
Designers: Figma link with comments on, not only the case study PDF—
Let people watch the thinking, not the myth.
Socials reward launches; your real people reward continuity—
A weekly "still drafting" note beats quarterly vanity.
AI projects: share the half-working agent and the guardrails you added—
Fragments are how trust in new tools gets padded.
Tomorrow: post one fragment—no CTA, no launch language—
Just "here's a piece." Feel the pressure un-bandage.
[Bridge]
The launch is a door...
The draft is the hallway...
People live in hallways...
More than they admit, always.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So stop saving every share for the day you're "ready,"
Share fragments and drafts—keep the signal steady.
Kleon's rule is anti-spectacle, pro-human pace—
Show the WIP. Invite the right eyes into the space.
One fragment this week. One draft that isn't finished...
That's how Show Your Work stays distinguished.
Launch when you launch—but live in the middle out loud...
Fragments first. The crowd will still be around.

headline: Tell stories about how work is made
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon in Show Your Work—
Tell stories about how work is made. That's the spark.
Not just the object—the tale—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Tell the story of how the work got made,
The detours, the doubts, the luck, the trade.
People don't only buy the thing—they buy the path—
Narrative is craft sitting next to the math.
How it was made is half of what it means—
Story turns a product into a scene.
Tell stories about how work is made—speak the arc—
From blank to done, leave a human mark.
[Verse 1]
Kleon says humans are storytelling animals first—
We remember origin more than feature lists rehearsed.
"Where did this come from?" is the question that sticks—
Influences, accidents, the night you almost quit.
A finished piece without a story is a mute object;
With a story it becomes a relationship project.
He points to credits, liner notes, "making of" docs—
The culture always craved the backstage clocks.
Your job isn't only to produce; it's to narrate
Without lying, without turning pain into cheap bait.
Structure helps: once upon a problem, then attempts,
Then the turn, then what you'd still attempt.
Steal structure from good essays, good case studies, good songs—
Beginning, middle, change—keep it not-too-long.
Show Your Work is storytelling as creative strategy—
Generosity with plot, not just gallery.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Portfolio site with only pretty finals? Add three paragraphs—
What broke, what you stole, what you'd do if you had half
The time again. Startup demo day? Lead with the user moment,
Not the slide that says "synergy" like an opponent.
Teacher's new unit? Tell the story of the lesson that failed first—
Kids lean in when adults narrate the worst.
Etsy, Gumroad, local market: tag the "why I made this" card—
Story sells trust better than a stock photo hard sell.
Engineers: architecture decision records are stories—
Write them so future-you isn't guessing categories.
TikTok "get ready with me" works because process is plot—
Apply that to code, cooking, counseling—the lot.
AI art? The story is human choices in the loop—
Without it, it's just another generated soup.
Tomorrow: pick one finished thing and write its making in 200 words—
Conflict, attempt, turn. No jargon herds.
[Bridge]
Facts inform...
Stories attach...
How it was made...
Is how people catch.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So stop treating narrative as marketing fluff on the side,
Tell stories about how work is made—with pride and with hide
The ego enough to include the misses.
Kleon's point: story is part of the practice.
One making-of note this week. True, short, alive...
That's how your work survives the scroll and thrives.
The thing and the tale—ship both, same day...
How it was made—that's the way.

headline: Teach what you know to clarify skill
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon in Show Your Work—
Teach what you know to clarify skill. That's the quirk
That levels you up—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Teach what you know—watch your own skill sharpen,
Explaining is a gym where weak spots start startin'
To show. Teaching ain't only for masters with crowns—
It's how the teacher finally gets the concept down.
Share the lesson, gain the clarity twice—
Once when you learn, again when you make it nice
For someone else. Teach what you know—donate the map—
Your craft gets cleaner in the handoff lap.
[Verse 1]
Kleon leans on a truth every good coach knows:
You don't fully understand until the teaching shows
The holes. Feynman technique before Feynman was a meme—
Simple words, no hiding behind jargon steam.
Show Your Work treats teaching as a creative act,
Not a side hustle only—though it can be that.
When you teach, you organize the mess in your head,
Sequence the steps, kill the parts that misled.
You also join a lineage of people who paid forward—
Workshops, blogs, office hours, blackboards.
Humility required: "here's what I know so far,"
Not "bow to my expertise from a distant star."
Teaching filters your audience to people who care
About practice, not just applause in the air.
Clarify skill by giving it away in plain speech—
Paradox of craft: the more you teach, the more you reach
Yourself. That's the loop Kleon wants you inside—
Document, instruct, refine, then re-apply.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Learned a Excel trick at work? Five-minute Loom for the team—
You'll remember it forever; they'll stop asking the same theme.
New parent figured a bedtime flow? Write the steps for a friend—
Teaching calms your own chaos when the night won't end.
Guitarist on day ninety? Post "three chords I wish I knew first"—
Beginners will love you; your fretting will reverse
Into cleaner form because you had to demo slow.
AI prompt skill? Run a lunch-and-learn—watch your standards grow
When someone asks "why that temperature?" and you must know.
Teachers teaching teachers: share the worksheet logic, not only the PDF—
The meta-lesson is where your skill gets depth.
Don't wait for a Udemy empire—teach one person well—
Office hours, Discord, family group chat—tell.
This week: pick one thing you can do that a friend can't yet—
Teach it in under ten minutes. Note what you still forget.
[Bridge]
To teach is to sort...
To sort is to see...
What you thought you knew...
Gets a second degree.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So stop hoarding half-knowledge like a dragon on a pile,
Teach what you know to clarify skill—go the extra mile
Of plain language. Kleon's generosity is a training plan—
Give the lesson; keep the sharper hands.
One mini-lesson this week. No perfection required...
Clarify by teaching. Get the skill rewired.
If you can explain it, you can improve it tomorrow...
Teach what you know—borrow that sorrow of "not yet"
And turn it into somebody else's on-ramp. You bet.

headline: Tend online presence like a garden
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon in Show Your Work—
Tend online presence like a garden. Don't jerk
From drought to flood—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Tend your presence like a garden, not a billboard war,
Plant steady, weed often, don't spray and ignore.
Seasons matter—growth is slow and real—
A garden feeds people; a spam plot makes them leave for real.
Water the roots: useful posts, true voice, kind replies—
Not fireworks every hour under metric skies.
Tend online presence like a garden—show up and prune—
Cultivate a place, not a cartoon.
[Verse 1]
Kleon rejects both ghost-town profiles and spray-and-pray,
He wants a durable place people return to someday—
A blog, a site, a consistent patch of internet earth
You own more than a rented algorithmic berth.
Gardens need weeding: delete the cruel, the fake, the clutter,
Update the bio, fix the broken links that mutter
Neglect. They need compost: old posts that still help,
Not only new buds chasing the current yelp
Of trends. And they need boundaries: not every pest deserves
Your water. Block, mute, protect the beds and nerves.
"Share your work" without "tend" becomes landfill content—
Tending is craft applied to how you're present.
Think of public parks vs. abandoned lots—
One invites a picnic; one invites you to not.
Your online self is a place. Act like a gardener with time—
Patient, seasonal, proud of dirt and design.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Pick one home base you control—site, newsletter, portfolio—
Socials are paths to the garden, not the whole show.
Post on a rhythm you can keep for a year without burnout—
Weekly essay beats daily panic shout.
Weed: archive the hot takes you're not proud of, fix the pin,
Update project pages so 2024 isn't still "in."
Compost: turn an old thread into a evergreen guide—
Gardens reuse nutrients; so should your site.
Comments and DMs: reply like watering cans, not firehoses—
A few real conversations beat a thousand empty poses.
AI can draft captions; you still choose what gets planted—
Don't let automation turn your beds into branded
Plastic flowers. Kids' accounts? Same metaphor, stricter fence—
Tend their digital dirt with consent and sense.
This month: one prune day, one plant day, one walk-through as a stranger—
Would you stay in this garden? Be the arranger.
[Bridge]
Billboards shout once...
Gardens feed seasons...
Presence is soil...
Not only reasons for applause and lesions of hustle.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So quit treating the internet like a slot machine only,
Tend online presence like a garden—slow, and lonely
Sometimes, and worth it. Kleon's image is gentle and strict—
Show up, share work, weed lies, let good things stick.
This week: water one old post, plant one new true thing...
Prune one vanity habit. Hear the garden sing.
Own a patch. Care for it. Invite people to sit...
Garden presence—that's the hit.

headline: Turn attention into patronage carefully
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon in Show Your Work—
Turn attention into patronage carefully. Don't force
The ask too early—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Attention can become patronage—handle with care,
Don't milk the room before the trust is there.
Give first, earn the right, then offer a way to pay—
Support is a relationship, not a trapdoor play.
Turn attention into patronage carefully—slow hand—
People fund what they already understand.
Careful over clever, service over squeeze—
Make the ask clean so the yes can breathe.
[Verse 1]
Kleon walks the line between free sharing and making a living—
Show your work, build an audience of the willing,
Then let them support without turning every post
Into a funnel that treats humans like a host
To parasites. Patronage is old: churches, courts, subscribers, fans—
People paying so the maker can keep making plans.
But rush the monetize button and you poison the well—
Attention was a gift; don't flip it into a hard sell
Before you've fed them. Sequence matters: be useful,
Be consistent, be human—then the ask is truthful:
"If this helps, here's how to keep it alive."
That's different from hostage content and fake scarcity dives.
He wants you to think like a good shopkeeper, not a pickpocket—
Clear prices, real value, no shame in the socket
When you need to charge. Art and money can share a table
If respect is the tablecloth and the terms are stable.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Newsletter free for a year of real help—
Then a paid tier with extras, not a paywall that yelps
On day three. YouTube: don't mid-roll every breath—
Earn watch time with craft; then sponsors that fit, not death
By twenty ads. Teachers selling a course: teach free samples first—
Patronage follows proof, not a launch-week thirst.
Open source maintainers: GitHub Sponsors after years of commits—
Careful means you've already given benefits.
Creators on Patreon: post the "what you fund" with honesty—
Hours, costs, boundaries—not a guilt symphony.
Don't confuse virality with readiness to charge—
A spike of strangers isn't a patronage barge
Until some stay. Count the people who return and reply—
That's your careful market. Look them in the eye
(virtually) and ask once, clearly, with an easy no.
Tomorrow: write your ask in one paragraph—value, price, why so.
[Bridge]
Free builds the room...
Trust fills the chairs...
Money keeps the lights...
If the ask is fair.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So share generously, then invite support without shame or trick,
Turn attention into patronage carefully—that's the Kleon pick.
No ransom, no fake countdown, no friend-guilt pitch—
Just: here's the work, here's the way to keep the switch
On. This month: one clear offer to people who already care...
Careful patronage. Clean air.
Give, earn, ask, deliver—then give again...
Attention to patronage—treat both like friends.

headline: Context—titles, tags, captions—is craft
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon in Show Your Work—
Context—titles, tags, captions—is craft. Don't scoff.
The frame is part of the art—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Context is craft—title, tag, caption, name—
How you label the work is part of the game.
A great piece with a lazy title still gets lost,
A clear frame helps the right eyes pay the cost
Of attention. Metadata ain't bureaucratic trash—
It's hospitality: "here's what this is, here's the path."
Context is craft—write the doorway well—
Help the work travel; help the story tell.
[Verse 1]
Kleon insists that putting work into the world includes how you package
The invitation—not clickbait sabotage,
But honest, sticky, findable language.
Librarians knew this; so did album liner sages,
Museum wall texts, newspaper hed writers at dawn—
Context is how strangers know what they're looking on.
A painting called "Untitled 47" can be fine in a white cube,
But online, silence is how good work gets screwed
By the feed. Tags are trails; titles are handshakes;
Captions are the first paragraph of the story you make.
This isn't selling out—it's finishing the job of sharing:
Make the work legible to people beyond your bearing.
Steal good context from poets of the subject line,
From teachers who name lessons so kids can find
Them next year. Craft the words around the thing
With the same care you gave the thing. Let both sing.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Rename that Google Doc from "final_final_v3"—
Call it what it does for a teammate in a hurry.
Portfolio piece: write a caption with problem, constraint, outcome—
Three lines. Tags that a hiring manager can actually plumb.
YouTube: titles that promise truth, not bait-and-switch shame—
Chapters in the description are context with a name.
Instagram: alt text and a caption that adds, not just "vibes"—
Accessibility is craft that includes more tribes.
Newsletter subject lines: specific over cute every time—
"Three PE timer mistakes" beats "You won't believe this" slime.
GitHub repos: README first paragraph is a title's cousin—
What is this, who is it for, how to run it—dozen
Seconds of mercy. AI-generated tags? Edit them hard—
Wrong context is worse than quiet. Be the guard.
Tomorrow: pick five pieces of your work and rewrite titles/captions only—
Watch discoverability and dignity both feel less lonely.
[Bridge]
The work is the meal...
Context is the table set...
Hungry people need both...
Or they forget and leave yet.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So stop treating metadata like an afterthought chore,
Context—titles, tags, captions—is craft at the core
Of showing your work. Kleon made it plain and kind—
Help people find and enter what you designed.
This week: one ruthless pass on names and frames...
Clear doors. Honest claims.
Write the doorway as carefully as the room...
Context is craft—give the work its costume of truth,
Not disguise. Title it like you mean it. That's the proof.

headline: A daily dispatch builds rhythm
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon in Show Your Work—
A daily dispatch builds rhythm. That's the hook.
Small daily share, strong pulse—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Daily dispatch builds rhythm—send the beat,
One small share so the practice doesn't retreat.
Not a masterpiece every morning—just a pulse check true—
Here's what I made, learned, saw, or worked through.
Rhythm beats inspiration that ghosts for weeks—
Dispatch daily; watch the muscle not get weak.
A daily dispatch builds rhythm—keep the drum—
Show up small so the big work can come.
[Verse 1]
Kleon loves the chain of daily-ish public practice—
A blog post, a photo, a sentence—simple tactics
That turn "I should share" into a habit loop.
Rhythm is kindness to future you: less regroup
Energy wasted restarting. Audiences also breathe easier
When they know when to look—predictable, not sleazier
Spam. Think newspaper columns, comic strips, captain's logs—
Culture ran on dispatches, not only on epilogues.
Daily doesn't mean exhausting; it means sustainable cadence—
If daily breaks you, find a dispatch you can maintain, friends.
The point is the metronome: creative life as practice hours,
Not only as rare concerts under confetti showers.
Share the ordinary workday so the myth of constant genius dies—
Rhythm is democratic. Anyone can try
A small send. Over months the archive becomes a body of proof—
You were here, you made, you told the truth.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Pick a channel and a tiny format you can keep—
One photo of the desk, one paragraph, one commit log peep.
Writers: morning pages private, one public line at lunch—
Dispatch without burning the private hunch.
Builders: end-of-day "shipped / stuck / next" in a team channel—
Rhythm for collaboration, not only personal panels.
Parents: a weekly family dispatch might fit better than daily—
Still a rhythm: Sunday note, what we made, what was crazy.
Fitness, language apps, streak culture—steal the good part only—
The streak serves the craft, not the app's lonely
Addiction design. If you miss a day, don't drama-quit—
Resume tomorrow. Rhythm forgives if you commit
To return. AI can draft a dispatch; you still must live a day worth noting—
Empty automated posts kill the reason you're boasting.
Tomorrow: send one true dispatch under five minutes—done is the doctrine—
Same time tomorrow. Feel the metronome lock in.
[Bridge]
Inspiration is weather...
Rhythm is climate...
Dispatch is the drum...
That keeps the day honest and tight.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So stop waiting for a big announcement energy spike,
A daily dispatch builds rhythm—Kleon's type
Of longevity move. Small, honest, repeatable share...
That's how Show Your Work becomes air
You breathe, not a project you dread.
Start tonight: one dispatch, then to bed.
Keep the drum for thirty days. Adjust the volume, not the vow...
Daily dispatch—build the rhythm now.

headline: Prefer durable notes over hot takes
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon in Show Your Work—
Prefer durable notes over hot takes. That's the torque
That lasts online—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Durable notes over hot takes—choose the long shelf,
Write what still helps when the outrage melts.
Hot takes burn bright and ash out by noon—
Durable notes are tools someone uses in June.
Prefer the evergreen, the checklist, the map—
Not the dunk that dies in the algorithmic trap.
Durable notes over hot takes—build the library—
Slow words that work. That's the real bravery.
[Verse 1]
Kleon's internet ethic leans toward useful and kind,
Not performative fury that empties the mind.
A hot take is a reaction dressed as wisdom for clout—
A durable note is something you'd still send out
Next year. Think field notes, how-tos, annotated links,
Reading logs, process docs—the stuff that thinks
In paragraphs, not pile-ons. He wants your archive
To compound: a garden of notes that survive
The news cycle. Hot takes train you to need enemies;
Durable notes train you to need clarity and memories
Of what actually works. Museums and wikis beat mobs—
Build the thing people bookmark, not the thing that robs
Attention for an hour. Steal the librarian mindset:
Catalog, connect, revise—leave the rage diet.
Show Your Work is a long game of public learning—
Hot takes are fireworks; notes are central heating.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Before you reply-all to the discourse of the day—
Ask: will this help a stranger in six months? If no, delay.
Turn the impulse into a durable note: "what I relearned about feedback"—
Not "person X is trash." Keep the craft, lose the attack.
Twitter/X, Threads, LinkedIn: post the template, the reading list, the mistake log—
Watch saves and DMs outlast the rage-dogpile fog.
Newsletters: one evergreen essay a month beats daily dunking—
Your future self will thank you when you're not stuck unpacking
Old cruelty. In teams: write the decision note, not the Slack storm—
Durable beats hot when the next hire needs the norm.
Parents in group chats: share the routine that worked, not the subtweet—
Kids' communities need tools more than heat.
AI makes hot takes cheaper than ever—so your edge is care—
Edited, sourced, useful, still true when the trend's not there.
Tomorrow: convert one opinion you have into a one-page durable note—
Steps, examples, limits. Delete the gloat.
[Bridge]
Heat is easy...
Light takes longer...
Notes outlive takes...
And make you stronger.
[Verse 3 / Outro]
So starve the hot-take habit and feed the notebook mind,
Prefer durable notes over hot takes—be the kind
Of poster Kleon would keep on a blogroll with pride—
Useful, human, built to be reused, not just eyed.
One durable note this week. No pile-on required...
Long shelf life. Real fire.
When the timeline forgets the fight, your note still teaches...
Durable over hot—that's how the work reaches.

headline: Design a make–share–rest weekly loop
song:
Yo, Austin Kleon in Show Your Work—
Design a make–share–rest weekly loop. That's the torque
For sustainable craft—let's flip it for today.
[Chorus]
Make, share, rest—loop it through the week,
Create, then show, then recover the peak.
No endless grind without the send or the sleep—
Three beats in the cycle is how you keep
The practice alive for years, not a burnout sprint.
Design the loop on purpose; don't improvise the hint.
Make–share–rest—weekly, human, clear—
That's the engine Show Your Work wants near.
[Verse 1]
Kleon's public practice needs more than inspiration speeches—
It needs a rhythm that includes the beaches
Of rest, not as laziness but as part of the craft system.
Make without share becomes a private prison
Of perfection. Share without make becomes empty content.
Rest without either is escape; together they're the solvent
That keeps creators from dissolving. Weekly is a human scale—
Days for deep work, a slot to ship a trail
Of process, and real off time that isn't fake scrolling rest.
Design means you choose the days; you don't let the feed request
Your life. Old wisdom: Sabbaths, studio days, gallery nights—
Culture always had loops; we forgot the rights
To stop. Show Your Work without rest turns generosity into debt—
You owe the internet nothing that costs your neck.
Build the loop like a product: simple, repeatable, kind to your body—
Make. Share. Rest. Then repeat with somebody
Watching or not. The loop is for you first—
Audience is a side effect of a sustainable thirst.
[Chorus]
[Verse 2]
Modern day apply it: Calendar three colors—make, share, rest—block them hard.
Monday–Thursday deep make if that's your card;
Friday dispatch: one fragment, one story, one teachable scrap;
Weekend actual rest: phone down, walk, nap—
Not "rest" that is inbox zero cosplay.
Parents: make during nap or after bedtime the honest way;
Share Saturday morning one photo to the right people only;
Rest means partner cover, not more content lonely.
Team leads: maker time protected, demo Friday, no-meeting rest buffers—
Same loop, org scale, fewer suffer.
Creators on every platform: one share hub per week max if you're drowning—
Rest is a feature. Stop frowning
At downtime. AI can help you make faster—don't spend the surplus
Only on more share; buy back rest on purpose.
This Sunday: draft your loop on one page—hours, channels, off limits—
Then run it one week. Adjust the minutes.
[Bridge]
Make without rest breaks...
Share without make fakes...
Rest without either waits...
The loop is what creates
A life you can keep...
